children.name: Incremental Array Builder, Items Per Row Only
children.characters: Incremental Array Builder, Items Per Row Only

children.name: Overview Arrays are collections of items organized in rows and columns, similar to Groupings, but are neatly arranged rows and columns and do not have a backing or border around them to signify that they are grouped.   We will mostly use dots as our items arranged in arrays, but real-world examples such as egg cartons and muffin tins will be displayed early on. For teaching, remediation, and certain activities, the rows or columns of the array can be highlighted for easier counting & emphasis. Arrays can be read as “X rows of Y items” or “Y columns of X items” so we will need multiple correct answers for some problems. We will also need the ability to rotate arrays 90° for teaching and certain activities. Students see the array before and after rotation and may compare it to another array.
children.characters:
Overview

Arrays are collections of items organized in rows and columns, similar to Groupings, but are neatly arranged rows and columns and do not have a backing or border around them to signify that they are grouped.   We will mostly use dots as our items arranged in arrays, but real-world examples such as egg cartons and muffin tins will be displayed early on.

For teaching, remediation, and certain activities, the rows or columns of the array can be highlighted for easier counting & emphasis.

Arrays can be read as “X rows of Y items” or “Y columns of X items” so we will need multiple correct answers for some problems. 

We will also need the ability to rotate arrays 90° for teaching and certain activities. Students see the array before and after rotation and may compare it to another array.

children.name: Types of Arrays For most array activities, we will display the content as an Expanded or Condensed Array.  Expanded Array: This is our default go-to visual for arrays. The rows and columns have ample spacing to clearly show the row and column highlights. Condensed Array: When there is not enough screen space for the Expanded Array, we instead use the Condensed Array, which has less padding between rows and columns. This is less readable so Expanded array is preferred, but it is useful for later activities that require us to show up to 10x10 arrays or multiple arrays at a time. Additionally, some early activities will teach arrays using real-world concepts like Egg Cartons and Muffin Tins. These visuals may use different icons and borders/backings to represent arrays. All arrays have toggleable highlights for their rows and columns, which are used by the guide or displayed in certain activities. These should appear behind the items in the area but in front of any backing or border elements.
children.characters:
Types of Arrays

For most array activities, we will display the content as an Expanded or Condensed Array. 
Expanded Array: This is our default go-to visual for arrays. The rows and columns have ample spacing to clearly show the row and column highlights.
Condensed Array: When there is not enough screen space for the Expanded Array, we instead use the Condensed Array, which has less padding between rows and columns. This is less readable so Expanded array is preferred, but it is useful for later activities that require us to show up to 10x10 arrays or multiple arrays at a time.

Additionally, some early activities will teach arrays using real-world concepts like Egg Cartons and Muffin Tins. These visuals may use different icons and borders/backings to represent arrays.

All arrays have toggleable highlights for their rows and columns, which are used by the guide or displayed in certain activities. These should appear behind the items in the area but in front of any backing or border elements.

children.name: Animations Highlight Animations: Rows slide in from the left one at a time from top to bottom, columns slide in from the top one at a time from left to right. Rows disperse one at a time from top to bottom. Columns disperse one at a time from left to right. Highlights can remain visible for an indefinite amount of time, for some activities the highlights will remain visible at all times. Only rows or columns should be highlighted at once, they should never both be visible. Rotate Animation: Arrows appear on the left and right of the array. Arrows can be larger or smaller depending on screen space. Clickable area for arrows is anywhere on the arrows or between them. Can be a large rectangle including the arrows and the array inside. Clicking initiates the rotation. Arrows fade away and do not rotate. Items in the array rotate position but do not rotate orientation. For example, if each item in the array was an up arrow, they would all rotate position but still be pointing up at the end of the rotation. The numbers making up the expression also rotate position but do not rotate orientation. The multiplication symbol does not rotate. After rotation, the expression may slide up to be closer to the array.
children.characters:
Animations

Highlight Animations: 
Rows slide in from the left one at a time from top to bottom, columns slide in from the top one at a time from left to right.
Rows disperse one at a time from top to bottom. Columns disperse one at a time from left to right.
Highlights can remain visible for an indefinite amount of time, for some activities the highlights will remain visible at all times.
Only rows or columns should be highlighted at once, they should never both be visible.

Rotate Animation: 
Arrows appear on the left and right of the array.
Arrows can be larger or smaller depending on screen space.
Clickable area for arrows is anywhere on the arrows or between them. Can be a large rectangle including the arrows and the array inside. Clicking initiates the rotation.
Arrows fade away and do not rotate.
Items in the array rotate position but do not rotate orientation. For example, if each item in the array was an up arrow, they would all rotate position but still be pointing up at the end of the rotation.
The numbers making up the expression also rotate position but do not rotate orientation. The multiplication symbol does not rotate.
After rotation, the expression may slide up to be closer to the array.

children.name: Screen Anatomy Array activities can have any combination of the following: 1-2 Arrays Row or Column highlights for each array Expression or equation below each array Rotation arrows Note About Expressions: The mathematical default for representing an array by expression is rows by columns (i.e. 4 rows of 5 is 4 x 5). Any expression without context should be interpreted as rows by columns, but 4 rows of 5 or 5 columns of 4 represent the same array and both are acceptable answers for the same array. This builds to the commutative property of multiplication (or turn-around facts) that 5x4 = 4x5. For many array problems, the answers 5x4 or 4x5 would both be acceptable. The caveat to this is problems where we first ask the student to choose whether they are writing their expression by Rows or Columns (see below). But when we show the array’s expression on screen, the default expression shown should be rows by columns, unless otherwise specified by the problem or lesson plan.
children.characters:
Screen Anatomy

Array activities can have any combination of the following:

1-2 Arrays
Row or Column highlights for each array
Expression or equation below each array
Rotation arrows

Note About Expressions:
The mathematical default for representing an array by expression is rows by columns (i.e. 4 rows of 5 is 4 x 5). Any expression without context should be interpreted as rows by columns, but 4 rows of 5 or 5 columns of 4 represent the same array and both are acceptable answers for the same array. This builds to the commutative property of multiplication (or turn-around facts) that 5x4 = 4x5.

For many array problems, the answers 5x4 or 4x5 would both be acceptable. The caveat to this is problems where we first ask the student to choose whether they are writing their expression by Rows or Columns (see below). But when we show the array’s expression on screen, the default expression shown should be rows by columns, unless otherwise specified by the problem or lesson plan.

children.name: Word Selection & Expression Builder This activity begins with an array and asks the student to choose between rows or columns. Hovering over “Rows” or “Columns” temporarily highlights the rows or columns in the array, respectively. Either rows or columns are valid paths for the student to take. After selecting “Rows” or “Columns”, the array slides up and remains permanently highlighted. The prompt at the top is updated to the second stage of the activity. The expression builder portion of the activity appears. This answer bank uses replenishing answers.   After filling all slots and checking for the correct answer, the slots/cards should fade away and the statement should condense to show the correct answer.
children.characters:
Word Selection &
Expression Builder

This activity begins with an array and asks the student to choose between rows or columns. Hovering over “Rows” or “Columns” temporarily highlights the rows or columns in the array, respectively. Either rows or columns are valid paths for the student to take.

After selecting “Rows” or “Columns”, the array slides up and remains permanently highlighted. The prompt at the top is updated to the second stage of the activity. The expression builder portion of the activity appears.

This answer bank uses replenishing answers.   After filling all slots and checking for the correct answer, the slots/cards should fade away and the statement should condense to show the correct answer.

children.name: Array Builder This activity begins with no visualization and instead, the student creates the array by building an expression. The first slot represents the number of rows, and the second slot represents the number of columns. When the first slot is filled in, highlighted rows animate in to show how many rows would be built. If the second slot is filled instead of the first slot, highlighted columns would animate in instead. If this card is removed, the rows/columns animate back out. When both slots are filled, an array with those specifications animates in. Replacing a number in either slot animates more rows/columns in or out. If a card is removed from a slot, the array animates out and just the row/column highlights animate in. Array Builder activities should always use Condensed Arrays to support answer inputs of 10x10 arrays.
children.characters:
Array Builder

This activity begins with no visualization and instead, the student creates the array by building an expression.

The first slot represents the number of rows, and the second slot represents the number of columns.

When the first slot is filled in, highlighted rows animate in to show how many rows would be built. If the second slot is filled instead of the first slot, highlighted columns would animate in instead. If this card is removed, the rows/columns animate back out.

When both slots are filled, an array with those specifications animates in. Replacing a number in either slot animates more rows/columns in or out. If a card is removed from a slot, the array animates out and just the row/column highlights animate in.

Array Builder activities should always use Condensed Arrays to support answer inputs of 10x10 arrays.

children.name: Double Array Builder After completing one array and checking for the correct answer, the array slides over and the student is prompted to make a different array with the same total number of items. At this point, the first array is locked, updating the expression on the right builds a second array on the right. After checking a second array, the guide compares the two arrays. If they are different shapes, we show them side by side and show their totals. If they are the same shape, a prompt appears with rotation arrows to turn the array. After turning the array, the identical arrays are compared. When answers 1-10 are provided, card size will need to be smaller (70px) to accommodate for the maximum possible size of arrays shown on screen.
children.characters:
Double Array
Builder

After completing one array and checking for the correct answer, the array slides over and the student is prompted to make a different array with the same total number of items. At this point, the first array is locked, updating the expression on the right builds a second array on the right.

After checking a second array, the guide compares the two arrays. If they are different shapes, we show them side by side and show their totals.

If they are the same shape, a prompt appears with rotation arrows to turn the array. After turning the array, the identical arrays are compared.

When answers 1-10 are provided, card size will need to be smaller (70px) to accommodate for the maximum possible size of arrays shown on screen.

children.name: Additional Activity Layouts Multiple Choice / Multi-Select: Condensed Arrays displayed as answers to multiple choice questions. Distractor answers could also be displayed, such as arrays missing dots (so invalid arrays) or scattered dots in no particular formation. Double Expression Builder: An array is displayed and the student must build both correct expressions that represent that array. Rotate & Expression Builder: An array is displayed and the student must rotate it, then build the expression that represents it. In this case, the numbers making up the expression do not rotate with the array, and instead fade out. Compare Two Arrays, Multiple Choice: Two arrays are presented and the student must identify if they are the same or different. Uses our basic multiple choice question styling. Property Application: Facts are provided, and the student must identify the correct answer. Styling should be similar to Word Problems but uses larger fonts and highlighted text for equations.
children.characters:
Additional Activity Layouts

Multiple Choice / Multi-Select: Condensed Arrays displayed as answers to multiple choice questions. Distractor answers could also be displayed, such as arrays missing dots (so invalid arrays) or scattered dots in no particular formation.
Double Expression Builder: An array is displayed and the student must build both correct expressions that represent that array.
Rotate & Expression Builder: An array is displayed and the student must rotate it, then build the expression that represents it. In this case, the numbers making up the expression do not rotate with the array, and instead fade out.
Compare Two Arrays, Multiple Choice: Two arrays are presented and the student must identify if they are the same or different. Uses our basic multiple choice question styling.
Property Application: Facts are provided, and the student must identify the correct answer. Styling should be similar to Word Problems but uses larger fonts and highlighted text for equations.

children.name: Update 1 A few more versions of Array problems that can appear: Drag & Drop, Two Statements: Two statements appear on screen (either descriptions or expressions) and the student must drag & drop to answer both. Array is static and does not update. Equation Builder with Static Visual: Static image that does not update, drag answers to complete the equation. Incremental Array Builder: Build an array by modifying the number of rows/columns and items per row/column. If both values are 0, no visual is shown. If Rows is more than 0, a number of highlight rows equal to the value is shown. If Items Per Rows is more than 0 and Rows is 0, a number of highlight columns appears equal to the number of Items Per Row. If both values are greater than 0, the array dots appear. Going back down to 0 in either category removes the dots and replaces them with the highlights. The total number of dots is a toggleable widget we can turn on or off for assisted learning depending on the lesson plan. Additionally, we may ask the student to solve this problem in two parts, first only presenting the rows control and have them set the correct number of rows, check, and then set the items per row and check. In that case there would only be one incremental set of buttons at a time.
children.characters:
Update 1

A few more versions of Array problems that can appear:

Drag & Drop, Two Statements: Two statements appear on screen (either descriptions or expressions) and the student must drag & drop to answer both. Array is static and does not update.
Equation Builder with Static Visual: Static image that does not update, drag answers to complete the equation.
Incremental Array Builder: Build an array by modifying the number of rows/columns and items per row/column. If both values are 0, no visual is shown. If Rows is more than 0, a number of highlight rows equal to the value is shown. If Items Per Rows is more than 0 and Rows is 0, a number of highlight columns appears equal to the number of Items Per Row. If both values are greater than 0, the array dots appear. Going back down to 0 in either category removes the dots and replaces them with the highlights. The total number of dots is a toggleable widget we can turn on or off for assisted learning depending on the lesson plan. Additionally, we may ask the student to solve this problem in two parts, first only presenting the rows control and have them set the correct number of rows, check, and then set the items per row and check. In that case there would only be one incremental set of buttons at a time.
